contest.title: 2025-2026 ICPC, NERC, Northern Eurasia Finals (Unrated, Online Mirror, ICPC Rules, Teams Preferred)

sources.notes: Official NERC tutorial; used to confirm the parity-only winning criterion for Fragmented Nim.

reference_candidates.notes: 洛谷题解候选来源；只有实际参考后才移动到 sources。

annotation.summary: Determine the winner of a fragmented Nim variant where each turn one player chooses a pile for the opponent to play on.
annotation.constraints: The full game tree is unnecessary; the winner follows from counting forced one-stone choices plus at most one larger-pile phase.
annotation.core_idea: The official argument shows that if Bob ever gives Alice a pile with more than one stone, Bob loses: depending on whether the state without that pile is losing or winning, Alice removes all stones or all but one and then forces Bob back into the same pile. Therefore both players must give the opponent one-stone piles while possible. After all one-stone piles are consumed, a remaining pile with more than one stone contributes exactly one decisive move; if no such pile exists, the player to move loses. Hence moves = count(a_i = 1) + [max(a_i) > 1], and Alice wins iff this number is odd.
tricks: ignore exact pile sizes except parity

solution_variants.name: main
solution_variants.summary: Count one-stone piles and add one if any larger pile exists; the parity of that count decides the winner.

tags.evidence: The final winner criterion is the parity of count(a_i=1) plus the indicator that any larger pile exists.
tags.description: Use even/odd parity as the decisive invariant or case split.
tags.created_reason: Parity-only game and construction problems are common and not captured by generic math/game tags.

tags.evidence: The solution proves which pile choices are losing/winning and then reduces optimal play to forced moves.
After any larger pile is chosen, the remaining state without that pile determines Alice's response and forces the game back to one-stone choices.
